votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if prod equals the product of the absolute values of a and b modulo 10.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, computing the product of the absolute values modulo 10 and comparing it to prod.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if prod equals the product of the last digits of the absolute values of a and b.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both compute the product of the last digits (obtained via modulo 10 of absolute values) of two integers. Python's abs(), %, and * operations on integers match Coq's Z.abs, Z.modulo, and Z.mul for all concrete integer inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: it computes the product of the last digits (obtained via abs and modulo 10) of a and b, and checks if prod equals this value. Python's abs, %, and * operations on integers match Z.abs, Z.modulo, and Z.mul in Coq for all concrete integer inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both compute the product of the last digits (obtained via modulo 10 of absolute values) of two integers and check if it equals the given prod value. Python's abs(), %, and * operations on integers match Z.abs, Z.modulo, and Z.mul in Coq for all concrete integer inputs.
Both compute prod as (abs(a) mod 10) * (abs(b) mod 10); with positive divisor 10, Coq’s Z.modulo and Python’s % behave identically for all integers.
Both compute prod == (abs(a) % 10) * (abs(b) % 10); Coq’s Z.modulo with positive 10 matches Python % on nonnegative inputs.
Both compute the product of abs(a)%10 and abs(b)%10; with positive modulus 10, Coq’s Z.modulo matches Python’s %, so results agree for all integers.
